title: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care

items.subtopic: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care
items.title: Economic Impact of Racial and Ethnic Health Inequities
items.content: The economic burden of racial and ethnic health inequities is substantial, reflecting the significant disparities in health outcomes among different groups. These inequities are quantified by their financial impact on the U.S. economy.
keyPoints: In 2018, the estimated economic burden of racial and ethnic health inequities was between $421 billion and $451 billion for adults with a 4-year college degree and between $940 billion and $978 billion for those without.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care

items.subtopic: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care
items.title: Quality of Care Differences Among Racial Groups
items.content: Research indicates that racial and ethnic minorities often receive lower-quality health care compared to whites, even when accounting for variables like stage of presentation, comorbidities, and insurance status. This disparity is critical as the U.S. health system shifts towards value-based purchasing.
keyPoints: Minorities may receive less care and lower-quality care than whites, despite controlling for confounders such as stage of presentation, comorbidities, and health insurance.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care

items.title: Life Expectancy Disparities by Race/Ethnicity and Socioeconomic Factors
items.content: Life expectancy disparities exist across racial/ethnic groups in the U.S., with African Americans consistently having lower life expectancy compared to whites and Hispanics/Latinos, even at similar education and income levels. These differences are influenced by socioeconomic factors, behavioral risks, and health care access.
keyPoints: African Americans have lower life expectancy than whites and Hispanics/Latinos across all education and income levels.
The largest historical gap in life expectancy between blacks and whites was 6.3 years for males and 4.5 years for females from 1975 to 2003.
Between 1999 and 2013, the black-white life expectancy gap decreased by 2.3 years (from 5.9 to 3.6 years overall).
Cardiovascular diseases and diabetes account for 35% of the male life expectancy gap and 52% of the female gap between blacks and whites.
Socioeconomic, behavioral/metabolic risk factors, and health care access explain 60%, 74%, and 27% respectively of county-level variation in life expectancy.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.title: Impact of COVID-19 on Life Expectancy Disparities
items.content: The COVID-19 pandemic exacerbated existing racial disparities in life expectancy, with the most significant declines observed among American Indian and Alaskan Native populations, followed by Hispanic and Black populations.
keyPoints: U.S. life expectancy declined by 2.7 years between 2019 and 2021 due to COVID-19.
American Indian and Alaskan Native people experienced a decline of 6.6 years in life expectancy.
Hispanic and Black populations saw declines of 4.2 and 4 years, respectively, compared to 2.4 years for whites and 2.1 years for Asians.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.title: Racial Disparities in Health Care Quality
items.content: Disparities exist in the quality of health care received by racial and ethnic minorities, even when controlling for insurance status, income, age, comorbid conditions, and symptom expression.
keyPoints: Blacks receive less optimal care than whites for conditions like pneumonia and congestive heart failure.
Blacks with end-stage renal disease are referred to transplant lists less often than whites.

Disparities include lower referral rates for cardiac catheterization and bypass grafting, less pain medication prescription, and fewer curative surgeries for lung cancer among blacks compared to whites.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.title: Trends in Racial and Ethnic Health Care Disparities
items.content: The 2022 National Healthcare Quality and Disparities Report highlights trends in health care disparities across various measures. While some disparities have decreased from 2000 to 2016–2018, significant gaps persist, particularly affecting poor and uninsured populations.
keyPoints: More than 400 health care process, outcome, and access measures tracked.
45% of quality measures show worse care for blacks compared to whites (85 of 190 measures).
47 out of 110 measures indicate worse care for American Indians and Alaska Natives compared to whites.
>90% of baseline disparity measures showed no improvement since 2000.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 2)

items.title: Disparities in Health Care Quality Among Ethnic Groups
items.content: The report identifies disparities in health care quality among various ethnic groups, with Hispanics, Asians, and Native Hawaiians/Pacific Islanders experiencing worse care compared to whites for over one-third of quality measures. However, Asians received better care for about 28% of these measures.
keyPoints: Hispanics receive worse care on 73 out of 190 quality measures compared to whites.
Asians and Native Hawaiians/Pacific Islanders also experience disparities in more than one-third of quality measures.
Asians received better care for approximately 28% of the measured quality indicators.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Racism on Health via Psychosocial Stress
items.content: Racism acts as a significant psychosocial stressor that negatively impacts health by causing hyperstimulation of the sympathetic-adrenal-medullary system and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis. This leads to vascular inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, neurohormonal dysregulation, and ultimately accelerates cardiovascular disease.
keyPoints: Racism is a form of psychosocial stress with biological impacts.
Stress causes hyperstimulation of the sympathetic-adrenal-medullary system and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis.
Results include vascular inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, neurohormonal dysregulation.
Consequences: acceleration of cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.title: Structural Racism and Health Disparities
items.content: Structural racism encompasses societal frameworks that perpetuate discrimination through sociopolitical, legal, economic, and health structures. This results in differential access to resources, influencing health disparities across generations.
keyPoints: Structural racism is a holistic framework explaining systemic discrimination.
It affects access to employment, banking, education, and healthcare.
Generational effects due to historical residential segregation.
Policies not addressing structural causes fail to resolve inequities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.title: Racial Bias in Clinical Algorithms and AI
items.content: Clinical algorithms using race as a factor, such as the CKD-EPI equation for estimating GFR, can inadvertently create disparities. Machine learning models may also perpetuate bias if they rely on historically biased data.
keyPoints: Race is not a reliable proxy for genetic differences in clinical algorithms.
CKD-EPI equation increases eGFR by 15.9% for black patients, affecting referral and treatment timing.
Cystatin C-based eGFR estimation avoids race-related bias.
AI models can perpetuate bias if based on biased historical data; must be assessed for fairness.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.title: Impact of Social Vulnerability on Health Outcomes
items.content: The CDC's Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) highlights how social factors contribute to health disparities. States with higher SVI scores show greater prevalence of negative health outcomes.
keyPoints: States in the highest tertile of social vulnerability have predominantly black and Hispanic adults, lower education levels, lower income, and higher unemployment rates.
Higher rates of hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, chronic kidney disease, smoking, and atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease are observed in these states.
There is a graded increase in mortality related to cancer, cardiovascular disease, and comorbid conditions in counties with the worst SVI.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.title: Health System Complexity as a Root Cause of Disparities
items.content: Navigating the U.S. health care system is complex, contributing to disparities in care for minorities and other vulnerable groups.
keyPoints: Even insured and educated individuals with high health literacy can struggle with the complexity of the U.S. health care system.
Groups at higher risk include those unfamiliar with Western health care models, with limited English proficiency, low health literacy, or mistrust in the system.
People of color are overrepresented among these groups, exacerbating racial/ethnic disparities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.title: Disparities Due to Health System Factors
items.content: Racial/ethnic disparities in health care are influenced by where and from whom minorities receive their care.
keyPoints: 25% of hospitals care for 90% of black Medicare patients, often showing lower performance scores on quality measures.
Health systems generally lack the infrastructure to measure, report, and intervene on racial/ethnic disparities in care.
Few hospitals or health plans stratify their quality data by race/ethnicity or language to address these disparities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Provider-Patient Communication on Health Outcomes
items.content: Effective communication between providers and patients is crucial for improving patient satisfaction, adherence to treatment regimens, and overall health outcomes. Sociocultural differences can lead to misunderstandings and dissatisfaction if not properly addressed.
keyPoints: 19% of all surveyed patients experienced problems with understanding doctors or feeling unheard; disparities exist among racial groups: 16% for whites, 23% for African Americans, 33% for Hispanics/Latinos, and 27% for Asian Americans.

Provider-patient communication without an interpreter is a significant challenge for patients with limited English proficiency, leading to misunderstandings in diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up plans.
Use of trained interpreters significantly improves the quality of patient-physician communication.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.title: Role of Clinical Uncertainty in Racial Disparities
items.content: Clinical decision-making is influenced by clinical uncertainty, which can lead to disparities when symptoms are expressed differently across cultural and racial groups. This may result in differential treatment even without prejudice.
keyPoints: Doctors' understanding of symptoms may vary based on the patient's race or ethnicity due to differences in symptom expression.
Clinical decisions might differ for patients from different racial/ethnic backgrounds, despite equal regard by the doctor.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.title: Influence of Stereotyping on Clinical Decision-Making
items.content: Stereotyping can affect clinical decision-making by simplifying complex information processing through social categories like race, gender, and age. This may lead to biased decisions even if the doctor is not consciously prejudiced.
keyPoints: Stereotyping involves using social categories to process information about others quickly but can be systematically biased.
Doctors might subconsciously use stereotypes in decision-making due to high information loads, potentially leading to racial/ethnic disparities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Stereotypes and Biases on Clinical Decision-Making
items.content: Stereotypes, influenced by societal messages, can unconsciously affect clinical decision-making. Doctors may develop perceptions based on race/ethnicity or socioeconomic status due to their training environments, leading to biased healthcare delivery.
keyPoints: Implicit biases in physicians are linked to disparities in treatment decisions, such as thrombolysis for myocardial infarction.
42% of surveyed physicians admitted that bias impacted their clinical decision-making.
Emergency medicine physicians showed the highest rate (62%) of admitting bias impacts due to high-stress environments.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.title: Differentiating Stereotyping, Prejudice, and Discrimination
items.content: Stereotyping is subconscious and can lead to lower-quality care if unaddressed. It differs from prejudice (conscious prejudgment) and discrimination (intentional disparate treatment).
keyPoints: Subconscious stereotypes may result in less patient-centered communication.
Patients perceive more biased physicians as being less communicative and empathetic.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.title: Mistrust in Healthcare Among Minority Populations
items.content: Historical factors like discrimination and medical experimentation have led to significant mistrust among minority populations towards the healthcare system, affecting their use of services.
keyPoints: 36% of Hispanics and 35% of blacks reported being treated unfairly based on race/ethnicity in past healthcare interactions.
65% of blacks and 58% of Hispanics fear future unfair treatment based on race/ethnicity.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Data Collection on Race/Ethnicity in Healthcare
items.content: Collecting, reporting, and tracking data by patients' race/ethnicity is crucial for identifying and addressing healthcare disparities. Despite improvements, many health systems still do not systematically collect this data.
keyPoints: 98% of 1083 U.S. hospitals collected information on race in a 2015 survey.
Only 45% of hospitals used race data to benchmark gaps in care.
The proportion of enrollees in plans collecting race/ethnicity data increased from 75% to 79% between 2008 and 2010.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.title: Routine Collection of Social Determinants of Health Data
items.content: The IOM recommends collecting a set of SDOH measures in electronic health records to provide a 'psychosocial vital sign,' enhancing understanding and addressing disparities.
keyPoints: IOM recommended 25 items covering domains like race, education, financial strain, stress, depression, physical activity, tobacco use, alcohol use, social connection, intimate partner violence, residential address, and geocoded census tract median income.
Data collection takes about 5 minutes and is deemed appropriate by both patients and providers.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.title: National Initiatives for Monitoring Health Disparities
items.content: Several national initiatives provide up-to-date data on health disparities, aiding in the monitoring and addressing of these issues.
keyPoints: The National Healthcare Quality and Disparities Report by AHRQ since 2003 tracks healthcare quality across various domains.
CDC's SVI database maps social factors for all U.S. Census tracts, updated every 2 years.
HOPE Initiative launched in 2018 benchmarks 27 indicators by race, ethnicity, and socioeconomic status.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.title: Impact of ACA on Insurance Coverage and Disparities
items.content: The Affordable Care Act significantly reduced the uninsured population, particularly benefiting racial/ethnic minorities. Medicaid expansion played a crucial role in this improvement.
keyPoints: Uninsured population decreased from 16.3% (2010) to 7.7% (early 2023).
Non-Hispanic blacks were 70%, and Hispanics nearly three times more likely to be uninsured than non-Hispanic whites prior to ACA.
Medicaid expansion accounted for an estimated 60% of the ACA's effect.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.title: Role of Evidence-Based Guidelines in Reducing Disparities
items.content: Adopting evidence-based guidelines can mitigate racial and ethnic disparities by ensuring uniform diagnostic and therapeutic options for all patients.
keyPoints: Clinical decision-making subjectivity contributes to disparities.
Evidence-based guidelines exist for diabetes, HIV/AIDS, cardiovascular diseases, cancer screening/management, asthma.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.title: Importance of Language Interpretation Services
items.content: Effective language interpretation services enhance communication and care quality for patients with limited English proficiency.
keyPoints: Lack of interpreter services leads to poor patient comprehension and adherence.
Supporting interpretation services improves effective doctor-patient communication.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.title: Importance of Cross-Cultural Education in Medical Training
items.content: Cross-cultural education aims to enhance providers' ability to understand and manage sociocultural influences on health beliefs, improving communication and care for diverse patient populations.
keyPoints: Up to 28% of senior resident physicians feel unprepared to handle cross-cultural issues.
70% of fourth-year medical students feel inadequately prepared for patients with limited English proficiency.
Incorporating cross-cultural education can improve communication and quality of care.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.title: Impact of Stereotyping on Clinical Decision-Making
items.content: Stereotyping by healthcare providers can lead to disparate treatment based on race or ethnicity, highlighting the need for education on how these factors may unconsciously affect clinical decisions.
keyPoints: The Liaison Committee on Medical Education mandates instruction on the impact of race, ethnicity, and culture in medical training.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.title: Increasing Awareness of Health Care Disparities
items.content: Efforts to raise awareness among physicians about racial and ethnic disparities in healthcare have been more successful than among the general public, with many physicians acknowledging their own biases.
keyPoints: In 2006, 60% of people believed blacks received equal care as whites; 50% believed Latinos did.
In 2005, only 24% of physicians disagreed that minority patients receive lower-quality care than white patients.
42% of physicians admitted personal biases impact their clinical decision-making.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.title: Practice Culturally Competent Care to Avoid Stereotyping
items.content: Health care providers should focus on developing skills for patient-centered care that respect cultural diversity, avoiding stereotypes by understanding the complexity of culture and individual patient needs.
keyPoints: Cultural competence involves compassion, empathy, responsiveness, and effective use of interpreter services.
Avoid stereotyping by learning about local communities while respecting cultural complexity.
Patient-centeredness includes qualities like compassion and empathy, crucial for all clinical encounters.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.title: Be Aware of Disparities to Improve Care Quality
items.content: Increasing awareness among health care professionals about racial and ethnic disparities is essential for monitoring behavior and ensuring equitable care quality.
keyPoints: Awareness leads to attunement in clinical practice, helping providers ensure high-quality care regardless of patient background.
Cultural competence has evolved from assumptions based on backgrounds to skills that enhance patient-centered care.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.title: Build Trust with Patients to Address Disparities
items.content: Building trust is crucial for addressing disparities, involving awareness of mistrust among minority populations and employing interpersonal skills that foster a solid doctor-patient relationship.
keyPoints: Mistrust impacts patient satisfaction and care; it's more prevalent in minority groups due to historical discrimination.
Trust-building involves reassurance, advocacy, honesty, openness, compassion, respect, and participatory decision-making.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care
items.question: Which of the following factors is NOT mentioned as impacting racial and ethnic disparities in health outcomes?
items.explanation: The passage mentions structural racism, SDOH, and access to care as factors impacting disparities but does not mention genetic predisposition.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care

items.subtopic: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care
items.question: What is the estimated economic burden of racial and ethnic health inequities for adults without a 4-year college degree?
items.explanation: The passage states the economic burden for adults without a 4-year college degree is between $940 billion and $978 billion.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care

items.subtopic: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care
items.question: Which of the following diseases is NOT mentioned as having poorer health outcomes among racial and ethnic minorities?
items.explanation: The passage mentions cardiovascular disease, cancer, and diabetes but not asthma as having poorer health outcomes among minorities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care

items.question: Which racial/ethnic group experienced the largest decline in life expectancy during the COVID-19 pandemic?
options: American Indian and Alaskan Native people
items.explanation: American Indian and Alaskan Native people experienced a decline of 6.6 years in life expectancy during the COVID-19 pandemic.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of county-level variation in life expectancy is explained by socioeconomic and race/ethnicity factors combined with behavioral and metabolic risk factors?
items.explanation: Socioeconomic, race/ethnicity, and behavioral and metabolic risk factors together explained 74% of the county-level variation in life expectancy.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.question: Which diseases account for a significant portion of the black-white differences in life expectancy?
items.explanation: Cardiovascular-related diseases and diabetes account for a significant portion of the black-white differences in life expectancy.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.question: What was the largest difference in life expectancy between blacks and whites from 1975 to 2003?
items.explanation: The largest difference in life expectancy between blacks and whites from 1975 to 2003 was 6.3 years for males.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.question: According to the 2022 National Healthcare Quality and Disparities Report, which racial or ethnic group received worse care than whites for about 45% of quality measures?
items.explanation: The report found that blacks received worse care than whites for about 45% of quality measures.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of quality measures showed no improvement since 2000 for those that demonstrated disparities at baseline?
items.explanation: For those quality measures that demonstrated disparities at baseline, >90% showed no improvement since 2000.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 2)

items.question: Which statement is true regarding Asians in the context of healthcare quality measures compared to whites?
options: Asians received worse care for more than one-third of quality measures.
Asians received better care for about 28% of quality measures.
Asians showed no disparities with whites across all quality measures.
Asians were the only group that did not show any disparities.
items.explanation: The report indicates that Asians also received better care for about 28% of quality measures compared to whites.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following best describes how psychosocial stress impacts health according to the passage?
options: It leads to decreased sympathetic-adrenal-medullary system activity.
It causes hyperstimulation of the sympathetic-adrenal-medullary system and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, leading to vascular inflammation and endothelial dysfunction.
It results in increased exercise and better adherence to medical regimens.
It has no significant impact on health.
items.explanation: Psychosocial stress negatively impacts health through hyperstimulation of the sympathetic-adrenal-medullary system and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, leading to vascular inflammation and endothelial dysfunction.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.question: What is structural racism as defined in the passage?
options: Discrimination based solely on individual racial prejudice.
A framework that explains how societal structures sustain discrimination through various systems, affecting health disparities.
The use of race in clinical algorithms to predict genetic differences.
A concept limited to historical events with no current relevance.
items.explanation: Structural racism refers to the ways society fosters and reinforces discrimination through sociopolitical, legal, economic, and health structures affecting access to resources that drive health disparities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.question: Why does the passage suggest avoiding race adjustment in clinical algorithms?
options: Race is a reliable proxy for genetic differences.
It can create inadvertent disparities by misestimating health risks.
All patients have uniform access to healthcare resources.
It ensures accurate predictions across all racial groups.
items.explanation: The passage suggests avoiding race adjustment in clinical algorithms because it is not a reliable proxy for genetic differences and can create inadvertent disparities in health care.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.question: What was the bias observed in the commercial prediction algorithm mentioned in the passage?
options: Black patients were overestimated as high-risk, receiving excessive clinical services.
White patients received lower clinical risk scores due to higher healthcare costs.
Black patients historically had less access to care and thus received lower clinical risk scores.
The algorithm was unbiased and fairly allocated resources.
items.explanation: The bias observed was that black patients, who were sicker on average due to less historical access to healthcare, received lower clinical risk scores and fewer additional services.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.question: Which report provided an important framework for conceptualizing and defining racial/ethnic disparities in health care?
options: Unequal Treatment: Confronting Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care
items.explanation: The 'Unequal Treatment' report by the National Academy of Medicine provided a framework for understanding racial/ethnic disparities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.question: What does the CDC's Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) measure?
items.explanation: The SVI measures social factors grouped into themes like socioeconomic status and household composition.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.question: Which factor is NOT a theme in the CDC's Social Vulnerability Index?
items.explanation: 'Healthcare system complexity' is not a theme in the SVI; it's discussed separately as a health system factor.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.question: What is one of the root causes for racial/ethnic disparities identified by 'Unequal Treatment'?
items.explanation: 'Health system complexity' is identified as a root cause for disparities due to navigation difficulties.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.question: Which group reported the highest percentage of experiencing problems such as trouble understanding their doctors, feeling that doctors did not listen, or having medical questions they were afraid to ask?
items.explanation: Hispanics/Latinos reported the highest percentage (33%) of experiencing these problems.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.question: What is a major consequence of provider-patient communication barriers for patients with limited English proficiency?
options: Frequent misunderstanding of diagnosis and treatment
items.explanation: Communication barriers lead to frequent misunderstandings of diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up plans.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.question: What is one factor that contributes to variations in clinical decision-making related to racial/ethnic disparities?
items.explanation: Clinical uncertainty can lead doctors to make different decisions based on their understanding of symptoms, which may vary among cultural and racial groups.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.question: What is the impact of using trained interpreters in provider-patient communication?
items.explanation: Physicians with access to trained interpreters report a significantly higher quality of patient-physician communication.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.question: What is a potential consequence of doctors developing stereotypes about certain racial or ethnic groups?
options: Doctors may offer more diagnostic and therapeutic interventions to these patients.
Doctors may begin to believe that 'these patients' do not like invasive procedures and thus may not offer them as options.
Stereotypes have no impact on clinical decision-making.
Doctors will always provide unbiased care regardless of stereotypes.
items.explanation: Doctors may begin to believe that 'these patients' do not like invasive procedures; thus, they may not offer these as options.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.question: What is the relationship between physicians’ unconscious biases and their clinical decisions?
options: Unconscious biases have no impact on clinical decisions.
Unconscious biases are related to differences in decisions to provide thrombolysis for a hypothetical black or white patient with myocardial infarction.
Physicians' conscious biases are the primary factor affecting clinical decisions.
There is no documented relationship between unconscious biases and clinical decisions.
items.explanation: Unconscious biases are related to differences in decisions to provide thrombolysis for a hypothetical black or white patient with myocardial infarction.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.question: In what type of environment are stereotypes most likely to be activated?
options: Environments where doctors have ample time and resources.
Stress-free environments with no multitasking required.
Clinical encounters characterized by stress, multitasking, and time pressure.
Non-clinical settings such as academic discussions.
items.explanation: Stereotypes tend to be activated most in environments where the individual is stressed, multitasking, and under time pressure—the hallmarks of the clinical encounter.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.question: What percentage of emergency medicine physicians admitted that bias impacted their clinical decision-making?
items.explanation: Emergency medicine physicians, who work in environments of stress and time pressure, topped the list by discipline at 62%.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following best describes a challenge in addressing racial and ethnic disparities in health care according to the passage?
options: The lack of appropriate systems to track and monitor racial and ethnic disparities.
Adequate collection of data on race, ethnicity, or primary language by all hospitals.
Uniformity in federal, private, and state-supported data-collection efforts.
Complete understanding of the disparities affecting minority groups other than African Americans.
items.explanation: The passage states that appropriate systems to track and monitor racial and ethnic disparities are lacking.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: According to the passage, what was a significant change in Medicare data collection that occurred in the mid-1980s?
options: Data on patient groups outside standard categories began being collected.
Data collection on race and ethnicity became mandatory for all patients.
The inclusion of primary language data started.
All hospitals were required to collect racial, ethnic, and language data.
items.explanation: In the mid-1980s, the Medicare database began collecting data on patient groups outside standard categories like 'white,' 'black,' and 'other.'
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: What does the IOM-recommended questionnaire for SDOH data collection include?
options: 25 items addressing various domains including financial resource strain and social connection.
Data on patient safety and quality of care only.
Geocoded census tract median income exclusively.
items.explanation: The IOM-recommended questionnaire includes 25 items covering multiple domains such as race, ethnicity, education, financial resource strain, stress, depression, physical activity, tobacco use, alcohol use, social connection or isolation, intimate partner violence, residential address, and geocoded census tract median income.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: Which organization has been compiling The National Healthcare Quality and Disparities Report since 2003?
options: The Institute for Diversity in Health Management.
Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality.
Geospatial Research, Analysis, and Services Program.
items.explanation: Since 2003, the Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality has been compiling The National Healthcare Quality and Disparities Report.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.question: Which factor was primarily responsible for the ACA's effect on reducing uninsured rates among racial/ethnic minorities?
items.explanation: Medicaid expansion accounted for an estimated 60% of the ACA's effect.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.question: What is a key recommendation to eliminate racial and ethnic disparities in health care?
options: Encouragement of the use of evidence-based guidelines
Decreasing minority representation in medical schools
items.explanation: The widespread adoption and implementation of evidence-based guidelines is recommended to eliminate disparities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.question: What challenge does the end of the COVID-19 public health emergency pose for Medicaid coverage?
options: Return to normal eligibility rules, risking higher uninsurance rates
items.explanation: As Medicaid returns to normal eligibility rules, many Americans are likely to transition out of coverage.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.question: What is a recommended intervention for improving health care delivery for patients with limited English proficiency?
options: Support for the use of language interpretation services
items.explanation: Supporting the use of language interpretation services improves communication and care quality.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.question: Which of the following is a focus area for cross-cultural education to improve health care providers' ability to manage diverse patient backgrounds?
options: Enhancing awareness of sociocultural influences on health beliefs and behaviors
items.explanation: Cross-cultural education aims to enhance awareness of sociocultural influences on health beliefs and behaviors.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.question: What did the Liaison Committee on Medical Education direct medical education to include?
options: Instruction on how a patient's race, ethnicity, and culture might unconsciously impact communication and clinical decision-making
Guidelines for reducing hospital readmission rates
Methods for improving electronic health record systems
items.explanation: The directive includes instruction on the unconscious impact of race, ethnicity, and culture on communication and clinical decision-making.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.question: What percentage of physicians in a WebMD survey admitted that their personal biases impact their clinical decision-making?
items.explanation: According to the passage, 42% of physicians admitted that their personal biases impact their clinical decision-making.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.question: What is an important first step in addressing racial and ethnic health disparities according to the passage?
options: Increasing awareness of these disparities among health care professionals and the public
Reducing healthcare costs for all patients
items.explanation: The passage states that increasing awareness of racial and ethnic health disparities is an important first step.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.question: Which approach is emphasized as important in addressing racial and ethnic disparities in health care?
options: Learning about cultural dos and don'ts for specific groups.
Increasing awareness of disparities among health care professionals.
items.explanation: Awareness of racial and ethnic disparities is crucial as a first step in addressing them.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.question: What has cultural competence evolved to focus on, according to the passage?
options: Making assumptions based on patient backgrounds.
Developing skills for patient-centered care and cross-cultural interactions.
Providing standardized care regardless of patient background.
Avoiding any form of communication with patients.
items.explanation: Cultural competence now emphasizes developing skills that include patient-centered care and are useful in cross-cultural interactions.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.question: What is a key strategy to counteract stereotyping among health care providers?
options: Assembling diverse teams with equal power.
Focusing on individual patient backgrounds only.
Avoiding discussions about race and ethnicity.
Providing care based solely on clinical guidelines.
items.explanation: Diverse teams with equal power can help prevent the development of stereotypes.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.question: Which factor is crucial in building trust between patients and health care providers?
options: Providing care without considering patient preferences.
items.explanation: Trust is built through shared, participatory decision-making and understanding the patient's background.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care
items.statement: Research has shown that racial and ethnic minorities receive the same quality of care as whites when confounders are controlled.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that minorities may receive less care and lower-quality care than whites, even when controlling for confounders.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care

items.subtopic: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care
items.statement: The economic burden of racial and ethnic health inequities was estimated to be between $421 billion and $451 billion.
items.explanation: The passage states this estimate for the economic burden of racial and ethnic health inequities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care

items.statement: Place does not significantly impact health outcomes.
items.explanation: Place matters for health, as demonstrated by geographic life expectancy variation at the census tract level.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.statement: Disparities in health care quality persist even when controlling for insurance status and income.
items.explanation: Racial and ethnic disparities in the quality of care have been documented even after accounting for factors like insurance status, income, age, comorbid conditions, and symptom expression.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.statement: Disparities in healthcare have completely disappeared from 2000 through 2016–2018.
items.explanation: The report found that some disparities were getting smaller, but many persisted and some even worsened.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 2)

items.statement: For about one-third of quality measures, Hispanics received worse care than whites.
items.explanation: The report states that for more than one-third of quality measures, Hispanics received worse care than whites.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 2)

items.statement: Race is a biologically founded category that explains health disparities.
items.explanation: The passage states that race has no biological foundation and is instead a product of historical racism.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.statement: Machine learning algorithms are inherently free from bias.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that machine learning algorithms can reflect preexisting disparities and should be assessed for accuracy and fairness.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.statement: The SVI includes genetic predispositions as one of its social factors.
items.explanation: SVI focuses on social factors, not genetic predispositions.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.statement: The biopsychosocial model integrates social and physical environment characteristics with individual attributes.
items.explanation: This integration is a key aspect of the biopsychosocial model.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.statement: Provider-patient communication is linked to improved health outcomes.
items.explanation: Effective provider-patient communication leads to better patient satisfaction, regimen adherence, and health outcomes.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.statement: Stereotyping in clinical decision-making is always conscious.
items.explanation: Stereotyping can be subconscious and people may not be aware of their attitudes or consciously endorse specific stereotypes.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.statement: Prejudice is a subconscious process that leads to stereotyping.
items.explanation: Prejudice is a conscious prejudgment of individuals, whereas stereotyping can be subconscious.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.statement: Mistrust in the healthcare system does not affect patients' use of services.
items.explanation: Mistrust greatly affects patients' use of services, leading to behaviors like 'doctor-shopping' and self-medication.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.statement: The collection of SDOH data in electronic health records is considered inappropriate by both patients and providers.
items.explanation: Both patients and providers see the collection of SDOH data as appropriate and important.

items.statement: The CDC SVI database is updated every two years.
items.explanation: The passage states that the CDC SVI database is updated every two years.

items.statement: The ACA reduced the uninsured population from 16.3% in 2010 to 7.7% by early 2023.
items.explanation: The passage states that the uninsured rate decreased significantly due to the ACA.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.statement: Minority faculty in medical schools are promoted at higher rates than their white counterparts.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that minority faculty are promoted at lower rates.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.statement: Most people surveyed in 2006 believed that blacks and Latinos received the same quality of care as whites.
items.explanation: The passage notes that nearly 6 in 10 people believed blacks received the same quality of care, and 5 in 10 for Latinos.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.statement: The majority of physicians in 2002 agreed that the health care system 'rarely or never' treated people unfairly based on race.
items.explanation: In 2002, 69% of physicians said the healthcare system rarely or never treated people unfairly based on racial/ethnic background.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.statement: Awareness of racial and ethnic disparities among health care professionals is not necessary for addressing these disparities.
items.explanation: Increasing awareness is an important first step in addressing disparities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.statement: Cultural competence now focuses on developing skills that include patient-centered care and are useful in cross-cultural interactions.
items.explanation: Cultural competence has evolved to focus on expanding skills beyond traditional patient-centered care.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care
items.assertion: Addressing racial and ethnic disparities in health care is important due to financial incentives.
items.reason: The shift toward value-based purchasing creates financial incentives and disincentives to achieve quality goals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why addressing disparities is important.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care

items.assertion: The gap in life expectancy between black and white populations decreased from 1999 to 2013.
items.reason: This decrease is attributed to improvements in health care access and quality for racial/ethnic minorities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason provided does not explain the decrease; it was due to a variety of factors.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.assertion: Cardiovascular-related diseases contribute significantly to racial disparities in life expectancy.
items.reason: These diseases account for over half of the gap in life expectancy between black and white females.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · NATURE AND EXTENT OF DISPARITIES (part 1)

items.assertion: Structural racism explains the persistence of health care disparities.
items.reason: It refers to societal structures that reinforce discrimination through various systems affecting access to resources.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why structural racism persists in causing health care disparities.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.assertion: Race adjustment in clinical algorithms is beneficial for all racial groups.
items.reason: It accurately reflects genetic differences across races.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because race adjustment can create disparities; the reason is also false as race is not a reliable proxy for genetic difference.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · ROOT CAUSES OF DISPARITIES

items.assertion: The SVI demonstrates that states with higher social vulnerability have predominantly white adults.
items.reason: States in the highest tertile of social vulnerability had predominantly black and Hispanic adults.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these states have predominantly black and Hispanic adults.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.assertion: Health systems are well-prepared to measure, report, and intervene to reduce disparities in care.
items.reason: Few hospitals or health plans stratify their quality data by race/ethnicity or language to measure disparities.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; few health systems are prepared as described.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 1)

items.assertion: Clinical decisions can vary based on the doctor's understanding of symptoms.
items.reason: Doctors may understand symptoms best when expressed by patients of their own racial/ethnic groups.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why clinical decisions might differ.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.assertion: Stereotyping in decision-making is always harmful.
items.reason: People use stereotypes to simplify information processing under cognitive load.
items.explanation: While stereotyping can be biased and problematic, it is not inherently always harmful as it helps process information quickly.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 2)

items.assertion: Historic factors such as discrimination have contributed to mistrust among minority populations.
items.reason: The exploitation of blacks during the Tuskegee syphilis study left a legacy of mistrust that persists today.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.assertion: Emergency medicine physicians have the lowest rate of admitting bias impacts their clinical decision-making.
items.reason: Emergency medicine environments are characterized by stress, time pressure, and multitasking.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; emergency medicine physicians actually had the highest rate at 62%.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 3)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.assertion: The Health Opportunity and Equity (HOPE) Initiative was launched in 2018.
items.reason: It benchmarks and tracks 27 indicators by race, ethnicity, and socioeconomic status.
items.explanation: Both the assertion about HOPE's launch year and its purpose of tracking indicators are true.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.assertion: All U.S. hospitals collect data on race, ethnicity, and primary language for benchmarking gaps in care.
items.reason: A survey found that only a portion of hospitals collected such data to benchmark gaps in care.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because not all hospitals collect this data; the reason correctly states that only some do.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (part 4) | KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.assertion: Increasing the proportion of underrepresented minorities in the health care workforce is crucial.
items.reason: Minority students are still underrepresented in medical schools, which affects the diversity of the physician workforce.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why increasing minority representation is important.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.assertion: Medicaid expansion had no significant impact on reducing uninsured rates.
items.reason: Medicaid expansion accounted for an estimated 60% of the ACA's effect.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason correctly states Medicaid expansion's impact.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.assertion: Incorporating cross-cultural education into medical training will improve communication and care quality.
items.reason: Cross-cultural education enhances providers' skills in understanding sociocultural influences on health beliefs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.subtopic: KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)
items.assertion: Most literature on racial disparities focuses on black-versus-white differences.
items.reason: There is a need for research identifying promising practices to address these disparities.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why most literature focuses on black-versus-white differences.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · KEY RECOMMENDATIONS TO ADDRESS RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.assertion: Assembling diverse teams with equal power helps prevent the development of stereotypes.
items.reason: A sense of camaraderie develops, which prevents stereotyping based on race/ethnicity or culture.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why assembling diverse teams is effective in preventing stereotypes.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION

items.assertion: Patient mistrust of the health care system can be completely erased by historical acknowledgment.
items.reason: Historic legacy of discrimination impacts patient trust and satisfaction.
items.explanation: While historic acknowledgment is important, it cannot completely erase mistrust.
items.reference: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Health Care · IMPLICATIONS FOR CLINICAL PRACTICE | CONCLUSION
